mnemonics.title: Remember Modus Ponens
mnemonics.content: MP: 'If P then Q. P is true, so Q is true' - Think: 'Premise confirms, so Conclusion follows' (P→Q)∧P⇒Q

mnemonics.title: Remember Modus Tollens
mnemonics.content: MT: 'If P then Q. Q is false, so P is false' - Think: 'Tollens' sounds like 'toll' (take away) the consequent to deny the antecedent. (P→Q)∧¬Q⇒¬P

mnemonics.title: Remember Hypothetical Syllogism
mnemonics.content: HS: 'Chain rule' - If P→Q and Q→R, then P→R. Think of it like dominos: P knocks Q which knocks R. P knocks R directly!

mnemonics.title: Remember Disjunctive Syllogism
mnemonics.content: DS: 'One false, other true' - P∨Q and ¬P ⇒ Q. Think: Eliminate (syllogism) one option to find the other.

mnemonics.title: Remember Addition Rule
mnemonics.content: ADD: 'You can always add more' - From P infer P∨Q. Think: Adding choices never hurts!

mnemonics.title: Remember Simplification Rule
mnemonics.content: SIMP: 'Break it down' - From P∧Q infer P. Think: Simplify a compound to its parts.

mnemonics.title: Remember Constructive Dilemma
mnemonics.content: CD: 'Two conditionals + OR of antecedents → OR of consequents' - Like choosing door P or R, both lead to outcomes Q or S.

acronyms.usage: P→Q, P ⊢ Q - Affirming antecedent to affirm consequent
P→Q, ¬Q ⊢ ¬P - Denying consequent to deny antecedent
P→Q, Q→R ⊢ P→R - Chain rule for implications
(P→Q)∧(R→S), P∨R ⊢ Q∨S - Two conditionals with disjunction

visualTips.visualization: Draw an arrow P→Q, with P highlighted, then show Q automatically highlighted. Think: 'P pushes the button, Q must light up'.
Draw P→Q with Q crossed out, then show P automatically crossed out. Think: 'Q didn't happen, so P couldn't have triggered it'.
Three dominos: P→Q→R. Show that P hitting triggers R. Draw direct arrow P→R as shortcut.
Create table with all premise columns. Highlight rows where all premises=T. Check if conclusion=T in those rows.
Draw premises as boxes feeding into a funnel, conclusion as output. Valid = no truth-leak from T premises to F conclusion.

analogies.analogy: Like a cause-effect chain: Switch on (P) → Light (Q). If switch is on, light must be on.
Like broken appliance: Plug→Works. If not working, then plug not connected (or other cause).
Like transitivity in math: If A=B and B=C, then A=C. Same with implications.
Like a valid recipe: If you follow steps exactly (premises true), you MUST get the dish (conclusion true).
A valid recipe (valid argument) with fresh ingredients (true premises) = delicious meal (sound argument).
